title: Pressure Drop
description: Eb minor, 138 BPM, Ebm to B, 4-on-floor drums, a stab, and a Production Hut top-loop layer. This is the fastest track in the set and uses exact loop-layering so it feels bigger without guessing.
what_youll_learn: How to layer a warped Production Hut top loop over programmed one-shots
How i to bVI feels in Eb minor at 138 BPM
How to keep a fast mix tight when there is both a stab and a loop layer
How to build intensity through loop muting and return automation

steps.title: Set the session
steps.instruction: Create a new Ableton Live set at 138 BPM and save it as 'original-15-pressure-drop'. Make these tracks in order: DRUMS, LOOP TOP, BASS, PAD, STAB, and a return track named VERB. The song is Eb minor to B major, with one programmed kit plus one warped top loop.
steps.why: This lesson teaches controlled density: the song gets bigger through layering, but every added layer is still specified exactly.
steps.tip: The loop layer must support the one-shots, not replace them.

steps.title: Load the programmed drum kit
steps.instruction: Load a Drum Rack on DRUMS and place these exact samples: C1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 3/DRUMS ONE SHOTS/KICK/PH_UKG3_KICK_20.wav', D1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 2/One Shots/Clap/PH_UKG2_Clap_31.wav', F#1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 3/DRUMS ONE SHOTS/CLOSED/PH_UKG3_CLOSED_1.wav', G#1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 2/One Shots/Shaker/PH_UKG2_Shaker_1.wav'.
steps.why: The programmed kit gives you exact low-end and backbeat control. The loop will only provide extra top movement later.
steps.tip: Keep the one-shots dry and clean. The loop layer will bring enough texture.

steps.title: Program the one-shot drum pattern
steps.instruction: Create a 2-bar DRUMS clip. All note lengths are 0.25 beats. KICK C1: 1.1.1 vel 96, 1.2.1 vel 90, 1.3.1 vel 95, 1.4.1 vel 88, 2.1.1 vel 96, 2.2.1 vel 90, 2.3.1 vel 95, 2.4.1 vel 88. CLAP D1: 1.2.1 vel 91, 1.4.1 vel 89, 2.2.1 vel 91, 2.4.1 vel 89. CLOSED HAT F#1: 1.1.3 vel 64, 1.2.3 vel 62, 1.3.3 vel 66, 1.4.3 vel 61, 2.1.3 vel 64, 2.2.3 vel 62, 2.3.3 vel 67, 2.4.3 vel 60. SHAKER G#1: 1.2.4 vel 43, 1.4.4 vel 41, 2.2.4 vel 43, 2.4.4 vel 41. Group DRUMS and add Glue Compressor with Ratio 4:1, Attack 3 ms, Release Auto, Threshold -5 dB, Soft Clip On.
steps.why: The one-shots create the exact skeleton of the groove. The loop will only widen the hat and percussion feel around that skeleton.
steps.tip: The loop should never be the only reason the groove works.

steps.title: Add and warp the loop layer
steps.instruction: Drag 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 3/DRUM LOOPS/135BPM DRUM LOOPS/PH_UKG3_Loop_Freedom_Top.wav' onto the LOOP TOP track. Turn Warp On. Set Seg. BPM to 135.00. Set Warp Mode to Beats. Set Preserve to Transients and Loop Length to 2 bars. Duplicate the loop so it can cover all full-drop sections later. Set LOOP TOP track fader to -18.0 dB and High-Pass it with EQ Eight at 300 Hz, 24 dB slope.
steps.why: Using a top loop instead of a full loop means the programmed kick and clap stay in charge while the hats and percussion become more alive and human-feeling.
steps.tip: If the loop makes the low end worse, the high-pass is not high enough.

steps.title: Write the bass line
steps.instruction: On BASS load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Sub- Turn The Page'. Leave it exactly as loaded. Create a 4-bar clip with these notes: Eb1 at 1.1.1, length 1.5 beats, velocity 92; Bb1 at 1.2.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 74; Eb1 at 1.3.1, length 1.0 beats, velocity 90; Gb1 at 1.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 72; B0 at 2.1.1, length 1.5 beats, velocity 89; F#1 at 2.2.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 72; B0 at 2.3.1, length 1.0 beats, velocity 87; D#1 at 2.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 70; Eb1 at 3.1.1, length 1.5 beats, velocity 92; Bb1 at 3.2.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 74; Eb1 at 3.3.1, length 1.0 beats, velocity 90; Gb1 at 3.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 72; B0 at 4.1.1, length 2.5 beats, velocity 89; F#1 at 4.3.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 70; D1 at 4.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 66.
steps.why: The fast tempo needs a bass line that is clear but not static. These pickups give energy without sacrificing the root movement.
steps.tip: The final D1 is the forward-pull note into the next Eb bar. Keep it short.

steps.title: Process the bass
steps.instruction: After Serum add Saturator, Compressor, EQ Eight, and Utility. Saturator: Drive 4.6 dB, Soft Clip On, Output -2.8 dB. Compressor: Sidechain On, Audio From DRUMS, Threshold -18 dB, Ratio 4:1, Attack 0.30 ms, Release 76 ms. EQ Eight: High-Pass 30 Hz at 24 dB slope. Utility: Width 0 percent. Set BASS fader to -5.0 dB.
steps.why: The fastest tempo in the set needs the fastest bass recovery. The shorter release keeps the bass from smearing over the kick.
steps.tip: This bass should feel tight and pressurized, not soft.

steps.title: Load the pad and chords
steps.instruction: On PAD load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Pad - Emosh'. Leave the preset exactly as loaded. Your two chords are Eb minor = Eb, Gb, Bb and B major = B, D#, F#.
steps.why: The major bVI chord is the bright flash in this track. The speed makes that flash feel urgent instead of dreamy.
steps.tip: The pad should still sound emotional even though the song is fast.

steps.title: Program the pad, return reverb, and sidechain
steps.instruction: Create a 4-bar PAD clip. Draw Gb3 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 72; Bb3 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 68; Eb4 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 64; D#4 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 72; F#4 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 68; B4 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 64; Gb3 at 3.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 72; Bb3 at 3.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 68; Eb4 at 3.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 64; D#4 at 4.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 72; F#4 at 4.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 68; B4 at 4.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 64. On VERB add Reverb with Decay Time 2.3 s, Pre-Delay 9 ms, Dry/Wet 100 percent, then EQ Eight High-Pass 220 Hz. On PAD add Compressor with Sidechain On, Audio From DRUMS, Threshold -22 dB, Ratio 4:1, Attack 0.10 ms, Release 120 ms. Set PAD send to VERB at -18.0 dB and PAD fader to -12.0 dB.
steps.why: Alternating the two chords every bar keeps the song urgent. The pad is emotional, but the sidechain stops it from slowing the track down.
steps.tip: If the pad feels too slow, it is usually too loud, not too sustained.

steps.title: Write the stab line
steps.instruction: On STAB load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Stab- Sam'. Leave the preset exactly as loaded. Create a 4-bar clip with these notes: Bb4 at 1.1.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 82; Gb4 at 1.3.3, length 0.25 beats, velocity 74; D#5 at 2.1.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 80; F#4 at 2.3.3, length 0.25 beats, velocity 72; Bb4 at 3.1.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 82; Gb4 at 3.3.3, length 0.25 beats, velocity 74; D#5 at 4.1.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 80; F#4 at 4.3.3, length 0.25 beats, velocity 72. Set STAB send to VERB at -20.0 dB and STAB fader to -14.0 dB.
steps.why: The stab gives the top half of the song rhythmic punctuation while the loop layer handles the extra percussive detail.
steps.tip: Do not lengthen these notes. The loop layer already adds tail and motion.

steps.title: Create the vocal pocket
steps.instruction: On PAD add EQ Eight after the Compressor: High-Pass 145 Hz, Bell 950 Hz at -3.5 dB with Q 1.50, Bell 2.5 kHz at -2.5 dB with Q 1.30. On STAB add EQ Eight: High-Pass 260 Hz, Bell 3.0 kHz at -2.0 dB with Q 1.30. On LOOP TOP keep the 300 Hz high-pass from step 4 and add a Bell cut at 6.5 kHz of -1.5 dB with Q 1.20.
steps.why: The loop can make the top end harsh if you leave it full-range. Small cuts keep the record energetic instead of fatiguing.
steps.tip: If the hats feel sharp, fix the loop EQ before you touch the one-shot hat.

steps.title: Arrange the full track with loop staging
steps.instruction: Build the arrangement exactly like this. Bars 1-8: intro, DRUMS only and no LOOP TOP. Bars 9-24: drop 1, DRUMS plus BASS plus PAD plus STAB, and turn LOOP TOP on only from bar 17. Bars 25-32: break, PAD plus STAB only, mute LOOP TOP entirely, and automate STAB send to VERB from -20 dB to -15 dB. Delete all clips on bar 32 beat 4 for a 1-beat silence. Bars 33-48: drop 2A, full arrangement including LOOP TOP from the start. Bars 49-64: drop 2B, keep everything on and raise LOOP TOP fader from -18 dB to -16 dB across bars 57-64. Bars 65-72: outro, mute STAB at bar 65, mute LOOP TOP at bar 67, mute PAD at bar 69, mute BASS at bar 71, let DRUMS finish bar 72.
steps.why: The loop layer is treated like arrangement fuel. Holding it back, removing it, and then returning it at exact times is what makes it exciting instead of constant.
steps.tip: If the first drop already feels huge enough, resist the urge to add the loop earlier. Its delayed arrival is part of the lesson.

steps.title: Set the mix
steps.instruction: Set DRUMS group to -4.0 dB, LOOP TOP to -18.0 dB, BASS to -5.0 dB, PAD to -12.0 dB, STAB to -14.0 dB, VERB return to -18.5 dB, and Master to 0.0 dB. Add Limiter on Master with Ceiling -0.30 dB.
steps.why: The loop must stay lower than the one-shots. It is support energy, not the main groove.
steps.tip: If the clap becomes harder to hear once the loop is on, lower LOOP TOP first.

steps.title: Export Pressure Drop
steps.instruction: Export bars 1 through 72 as WAV, 24-bit, 44.1 kHz with filename 'original-15-pressure-drop-v1.wav'. After export, compare bars 17-24 with bars 57-64 and confirm the later section feels wider and more urgent because of the loop-layer staging, not because the whole mix got uncontrolled.
steps.why: That tells you whether you layered the loop like a producer instead of just adding volume.
steps.tip: If the loop feels too obvious, lower it by 1 dB instead of muting it. Support layers often work best just below the point where you notice them.
